fact.text: Microsoft was founded on April 4, 1975, by Bill Gates and Paul Allen in Albuquerque, New Mexico.
The name "Microsoft" is a portmanteau of "microcomputer" and "software," coined by Paul Allen.
Microsoft's first product was a version of the BASIC programming language interpreter for the Altair 8800 microcomputer, known as Altair BASIC.
When Microsoft was founded, Bill Gates was 19 years old and Paul Allen was 22.
Bill Gates dropped out of Harvard University in 1975 to co-found Microsoft with Paul Allen.
Gates and Allen wrote the Altair BASIC interpreter without owning an Altair machine, using a software emulator they built on a PDP-10 mainframe at Harvard.
Microsoft's first office was in Albuquerque, New Mexico, near MITS (Micro Instrumentation and Telemetry Systems), the maker of the Altair 8800.
Microsoft was officially incorporated in the state of New Mexico on November 26, 1976.
Microsoft relocated its headquarters from Albuquerque, New Mexico, to Bellevue, Washington, in January 1979.
Microsoft's revenue in its first year of operation (1975) was approximately $16,005.
In 1980, IBM approached Microsoft to provide an operating system for its upcoming personal computer, the IBM PC.
Microsoft purchased 86-DOS (also called QDOS - Quick and Dirty Operating System) from Seattle Computer Products for approximately $50,000 in 1981 and licensed it to IBM as PC-DOS.
MS-DOS 1.0 was released in August 1981 alongside the original IBM PC, establishing Microsoft as a dominant force in the PC software market.
Microsoft retained the right to license MS-DOS to other PC manufacturers - a pivotal decision that allowed it to become the standard OS for IBM-compatible PCs.
Microsoft moved its headquarters from Bellevue to Redmond, Washington, in February 1986, where it remains to this day.
Microsoft went public on March 13, 1986, with an IPO price of $21 per share, raising approximately $61 million.
Bill Gates became a billionaire on October 28, 1987, at age 31, following the rise of Microsoft's stock price.
MS-DOS went through eight major versions from 1.0 (1981) to 6.22 (1994), each adding features like subdirectories, hard disk support, and memory management.
Microsoft released MultiPlan, its first spreadsheet application, in 1982 - a precursor to Microsoft Excel.
Microsoft Word was first released in 1983 for MS-DOS, originally called "Multi-Tool Word."
Microsoft announced Windows on November 10, 1983, billing it as a graphical extension of MS-DOS inspired in part by Apple's Macintosh interface. It was previously known as Interface Manager.
Windows 1.0 was officially released on November 20, 1985, after two years of development delays.
One of the people who worked on the first versions of Windows was Gabe Newell. He would later found Valve and release popular games and services, such as Half-Life, Portal, and Steam.
Windows 1.0 featured tiled (non-overlapping) windows, a mouse-driven interface, and bundled apps including MS-DOS Executive, Calculator, Clock, and Notepad.
Windows 2.0, released in December 1987, introduced overlapping windows, keyboard shortcuts, and improved support for expanded memory.
Apple sued Microsoft in 1988, claiming Windows 2.0 infringed the "look and feel" of the Macintosh GUI; the case was eventually decided in Microsoft's favor in 1994.
Windows 3.0, released on May 22, 1990, was the first commercially successful version of Windows, selling more than two million copies within six months of launch.
Windows 3.1 (1992) added TrueType font support, drag-and-drop functionality, and Object Linking and Embedding (OLE), and sold more than three million copies in its first two months.
Windows for Workgroups 3.11 (1993) added peer-to-peer networking capabilities, making it popular in small business environments.
Microsoft Excel was first released for Macintosh in 1985 and for Windows in 1987, eventually overtaking Lotus 1-2-3 as the world's dominant spreadsheet application.
Microsoft Office was introduced in 1989 for Macintosh as a bundle of Word, Excel, and PowerPoint - Microsoft had acquired PowerPoint's developer, Forethought, Inc., earlier that year.
Microsoft hired Dave Cutler, the chief architect of the DEC VAX VMS operating system, in 1988 to lead the development of a new enterprise-grade OS that would become Windows NT.
Windows NT 3.1 was released on July 27, 1993, introducing a fully 32-bit preemptive multitasking OS with a microkernel architecture, separate from the DOS-based Windows line.
The "NT" in Windows NT originally stood for "New Technology," distinguishing it from the consumer DOS-based Windows versions.
Windows NT 4.0 (1996) adopted the Windows 95 user interface and became widely deployed in corporate environments as a stable workstation and server OS.
Windows NT initially supported x86, MIPS, DEC Alpha, and PowerPC. Only the x86 version remained by the time Windows 2000 shipped.

The Windows NT Session Manager (SM) allows for additional subsystems to be executed. Up until Windows 2000, it supported OS/2 and POSIX subsystems. Afterwards, only the Windows subsystem was included.
The NT kernel was originally going to target OS/2, but, because of the success of Windows 3.0, Microsoft decided to make Windows its target.

The NT kernel would originally support the Intel i860 processor. Because of technical complications, such as a virtually tagged cache, it was scrapped as one of its targets. The i860 was codenamed N-Ten, making it another possible meaning for NT.

The only x86 machines that did not use a traditional BIOS firmware were the Silicon Graphics Visual Workstations 320 and 540. They required a separate HAL, which was included up until early builds of Windows XP.
The disk check application loaded on Windows startup is not CHKDSK, but AUTOCHK. It is one of the boot-execute programs loaded by the Session Manager.
The startup sound of Windows NT 4.0 lasts 10 seconds. Its shutdown sound is a reversed version of the startup sound.
Windows 95 was launched on August 24, 1995, with a massive marketing campaign that featured the Rolling Stones song "Start Me Up" and sold more than one million copies in its first four days.
Windows 95 introduced the iconic Start button and taskbar, which defined the Windows user interface paradigm for the next two decades.
Windows 95 introduced long file names (up to 255 characters), Plug and Play hardware detection, and 32-bit application support, while retaining DOS compatibility.
Internet Explorer was first bundled with Windows 95 (as part of the Plus! pack in 1995) and later shipped as an integral part of Windows 98, sparking antitrust investigations.
Windows 98, released on June 25, 1998, added native USB support, the Windows Driver Model, Internet Explorer 4.0 integration, and support for DVD-ROM drives.

At a 1998 Windows 98 demonstration, a notorious Blue Screen of Death (BSOD) appeared live on stage when a USB scanner was connected, in front of Bill Gates - becoming one of the most famous tech mishaps on record.
Windows Millennium Edition (Me), released in September 2000, was the last DOS-based version of Windows; it was widely criticized for instability and poor performance.
DirectX was first introduced in 1995 with Windows 95 as a set of APIs for handling multimedia and gaming tasks, becoming a cornerstone of PC gaming on Windows.
The browser wars of the late 1990s pitted Microsoft's Internet Explorer against Netscape Navigator, culminating in the U.S. DOJ filing an antitrust suit against Microsoft in 1998.
In 2000, U.S. District Judge Thomas Penfield Jackson ruled that Microsoft had violated antitrust laws and ordered the company to be broken into two separate entities.
The breakup order was reversed on appeal in 2001; Microsoft eventually settled with the DOJ in 2001, agreeing to share its APIs with third-party companies without being split up.
The European Commission fined Microsoft €497 million in 2004 for abusing its dominant market position, requiring it to offer a version of Windows without Windows Media Player in Europe.
Windows 2000, released on February 17, 2000, was the successor to Windows NT 4.0 and introduced Active Directory, the Microsoft Management Console, and improved hardware support.
Windows XP was released on October 25, 2001, unifying the consumer (9x) and enterprise (NT) Windows lines for the first time under the NT 5.1 kernel.

Windows XP remained in active use for over 12 years; Microsoft officially ended mainstream support in 2009 and extended support in April 2014, yet the OS still powered millions of machines years afterward.
Windows XP introduced the "Luna" visual style with the iconic green hill and blue sky default wallpaper called "Bliss," photographed by Charles O'Rear in Sonoma County, California.
Windows XP introduced ClearType sub-pixel font rendering, significantly improving text readability on LCD displays.
Windows XP Service Pack 2 (2004) was a landmark security overhaul that added the Windows Security Center, a built-in firewall enabled by default, and Data Execution Prevention (DEP).
Windows XP Media Center Edition, introduced in 2002, targeted home theater PCs and included the Media Center application for managing TV, music, and photos from a TV-style remote.
Windows XP Tablet PC Edition (2002) was the first version of Windows to support stylus-based handwriting recognition on tablet computers.
Microsoft launched the original Xbox game console in North America on November 15, 2001, entering the console gaming market dominated by Sony and Nintendo.
Halo: Combat Evolved was the Xbox's flagship launch title; Microsoft had acquired Bungie Studios in 2000 specifically to secure Halo as an Xbox exclusive.
Xbox Live, launched in November 2002, pioneered online console gaming with a unified online multiplayer service, voice chat, and downloadable content.
The Xbox 360 launched in November 2005, a year ahead of Sony's PlayStation 3, giving Microsoft an early-mover advantage in the seventh generation of gaming consoles.
The Xbox 360 suffered a widely reported hardware failure known as the "Red Ring of Death" (RRoD); Microsoft extended warranties and spent an estimated $1.15 billion addressing the defect.
Windows Vista was released to manufacturing on November 8, 2006, and made available to the general public on January 30, 2007, after a notoriously long five-year development cycle.
Vista introduced the Aero Glass visual style, featuring translucent window borders, live thumbnails in the taskbar, and the 3D Flip window switcher (Win+Tab).
Windows Vista was criticized for high hardware requirements, aggressive User Account Control (UAC) prompts, driver compatibility issues, and poor performance on low-end hardware.
Windows Vista introduced User Account Control (UAC) to reduce the attack surface of the OS by prompting users for explicit permission before allowing administrative changes.
Windows Vista introduced the Windows Side-by-Side (WinSxS) assembly store, allowing multiple versions of the same DLL to coexist on the system to prevent "DLL hell."
BitLocker Drive Encryption was introduced in Windows Vista Ultimate and Enterprise editions, providing full-volume encryption for system and data drives.
Windows 7 was released on October 22, 2009, and was widely praised as the successor Vista should have been, praised for its performance, stability, and refined user interface.
Windows 7 introduced a significantly redesigned taskbar with pinned applications, Jump Lists, and Aero Peek, consolidating taskbar buttons and eliminating the Quick Launch bar.
Windows 7 introduced Libraries, a virtual folder concept that aggregated content from multiple physical locations into a single unified view for Documents, Music, Pictures, and Videos.
Windows 7 introduced HomeGroup, a simplified home networking feature that allowed PCs on the same network to share files and printers with a single password.
Windows 7 Professional and Ultimate editions offered "XP Mode," a free virtual machine running a licensed copy of Windows XP for legacy application compatibility.
Windows 7 sold more than 240 million licenses in its first year, making it the fastest-selling OS in Microsoft's history at that time.
Windows 7 was the first Microsoft OS to include built-in multi-touch support, supporting gestures such as pinch-to-zoom and rotate on compatible hardware.
Windows 8 was released on October 26, 2012, featuring the radical "Modern UI" (codenamed Metro) that replaced the traditional Start menu with a full-screen Start Screen of live tiles.
Windows 8 controversially removed the Start button from the desktop taskbar entirely, drawing widespread criticism from users accustomed to the two-decades-old paradigm.
Windows 8 introduced the Charms bar - a hidden sidebar accessible by swiping from the right edge - housing Search, Share, Start, Devices, and Settings.
Windows 8 introduced the Windows Store (now Microsoft Store), a curated marketplace for Modern UI (Metro) apps, marking Microsoft's push into the app-store model.
Windows 8.1, released in October 2013, restored the Start button (though clicking it still went to the Start Screen), added boot-to-desktop option, and improved mouse and keyboard navigation.
Windows 8 and its companion version Windows RT extended the NT kernel to ARM processors, debuting on the original Microsoft Surface tablet.
Microsoft launched the Surface tablet line in October 2012, marking the company's entry into the first-party hardware PC market and signaling a shift toward devices and services.
Bill Gates served as CEO of Microsoft from its founding in 1975 until January 2000, when he stepped aside as CEO to focus on software architecture as Chief Software Architect.
Steve Ballmer became Microsoft's CEO in January 2000 and served until February 2014, overseeing the launch of Windows XP, Vista, 7, 8, the Xbox, and the company's push into cloud services.

Satya Nadella became Microsoft's third CEO on February 4, 2014, ushering in a cultural and strategic transformation centered on cloud computing, open-source collaboration, and mobile-first, cloud-first vision.
Bill Gates stepped down as Microsoft's Chairman of the Board in 2014, the same year Satya Nadella became CEO; Gates remained on the board as a technology advisor until March 2020.
Paul Allen left Microsoft in 1983 after being diagnosed with Hodgkin's lymphoma; he remained a shareholder and later became a prominent philanthropist and investor until his death in October 2018.
Windows 10 was released on July 29, 2015, and was offered as a free upgrade to users of genuine Windows 7 and 8.1 for one year after launch.
Windows 10 brought back a hybrid Start menu combining the traditional menu list with live tiles from Windows 8, addressing one of the most criticized aspects of Windows 8.
Windows 10 introduced Cortana, Microsoft's digital voice assistant, as an integrated component of the OS, competing with Apple's Siri and Google Assistant.
Windows 10 shipped with Microsoft Edge as the default browser, replacing Internet Explorer after more than 20 years; Edge was rebuilt on the Chromium engine in 2020.
Windows 10 introduced a "Windows as a Service" model, delivering feature updates twice a year through the Windows Update mechanism rather than paid major releases.
Windows 10 introduced native virtual desktop support via Task View (Win+Tab), a feature long present in macOS and Linux desktop environments.
Windows 10 shipped with DirectX 12, enabling lower-level GPU access for game developers, closer to the hardware than prior DirectX versions, leading to better performance and efficiency.
Windows 10 Anniversary Update (2016) introduced the Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL), allowing users to run a Linux environment natively within Windows without a virtual machine.
Windows 10 introduced Windows Hello, a biometric authentication framework supporting face recognition, fingerprint scanning, and iris scanning as alternatives to passwords.
Windows 10 replaced the Windows 8 Charms bar with the Action Center - a notification tray on the right side of the screen housing notifications and Quick Action buttons.
Windows 10 reached 75 million installs in its first month - the fastest adoption rate of any version of Windows at that time - aided significantly by the free upgrade offer.
Microsoft Azure was announced in October 2008 and commercially launched in February 2010 as Windows Azure, later rebranded to Microsoft Azure in 2014.
Under Satya Nadella, Azure became Microsoft's primary growth engine; by 2023, Microsoft's Intelligent Cloud segment (led by Azure) generated over $87 billion in annual revenue.
Microsoft launched Office 365 in June 2011, transitioning from perpetual software licenses to a subscription-based cloud model for its productivity suite.
Office 365 was rebranded as Microsoft 365 in April 2020, reflecting the broader inclusion of Windows, security, and device management tools beyond the core Office apps.
Microsoft acquired Hotmail in December 1997 for an estimated $400 million; it was one of the first major web-based email services and was later relaunched as Outlook.com in 2012.
Microsoft acquired Skype in May 2011 for $8.5 billion - the largest acquisition in Microsoft's history at the time - gaining 170 million users and a major presence in video calling.

Microsoft acquired Nokia's mobile phone division in April 2014 for approximately €5.44 billion, an attempt to gain traction in the smartphone market that largely failed, resulting in massive write-downs by 2015.
Microsoft acquired LinkedIn in December 2016 for $26.2 billion - its largest acquisition at that time - integrating the professional network with Office 365 and Dynamics.
Microsoft acquired GitHub, the world's largest code hosting platform, in October 2018 for $7.5 billion, signaling the company's deep commitment to open-source software.
Microsoft completed its acquisition of Nuance Communications, a leader in conversational AI and speech recognition, in March 2022 for $19.7 billion.

Microsoft completed its acquisition of Activision Blizzard, one of the world's largest video game publishers (Call of Duty, World of Warcraft, Candy Crush), in October 2023 for approximately $68.7 billion - the largest gaming acquisition in history.
Windows Server 2003, released April 24, 2003, built on the Windows XP/NT 5.2 kernel and introduced improved Active Directory, IIS 6.0, and enhanced group policy management.
Windows Server 2008, released February 27, 2008, introduced Server Core (a minimal headless installation option), Hyper-V virtualization, and PowerShell as a core management tool.
Hyper-V, Microsoft's type-1 hypervisor for server virtualization, was introduced with Windows Server 2008 and is now also included in Windows 10 and 11 Pro/Enterprise editions.
Windows Server 2012, released September 4, 2012, brought the Metro-era tile interface to server management and introduced Storage Spaces, SMB 3.0, and improved Hyper-V.
Windows Server 2016 introduced Nano Server, Containers, Shielded Virtual Machines, and Windows Defender as a built-in service, reflecting strong security and cloud alignment.
Windows Server 2019 added Windows Admin Center, Storage Migration Service, System Insights for predictive analytics, and deeper Azure hybrid integration features.
Windows Server 2022, released in August 2021, added Secured-core server capabilities, SMB over QUIC for WAN file sharing, and improved Azure Arc integration for hybrid cloud management.
Microsoft announced Windows 11 on June 24, 2021, ending speculation that Windows 10 would be the "last version" of Windows.
Windows 11 was released on October 5, 2021, as a free upgrade to eligible Windows 10 devices.
Windows 11 requires a Trusted Platform Module (TPM) version 2.0, a 64-bit dual-core 1 GHz+ processor, and Secure Boot support - requirements that rendered many older PCs ineligible for the upgrade.
Windows 11 moved the Start button and taskbar icons to the center of the taskbar by default, a major visual departure from all prior Windows versions.
Windows 11 introduced Snap Layouts and Snap Groups, allowing users to hover over the maximize button to choose predefined window arrangement templates on screen.
Windows 11 added the ability to run Android apps natively through the Amazon Appstore and Windows Subsystem for Android (WSA), though this feature was later discontinued in March 2025.
Windows 11 integrated Microsoft Teams directly into the taskbar via the Chat button, the deepest integration of a communications app into Windows to date.
Windows 11 introduced a refreshed UI with rounded window corners, Mica material effects (translucent backgrounds that adapt to wallpaper color), and a new Fluent Design icon set.
Windows 11 introduced DirectStorage, a gaming API that allows GPUs to load game assets directly from NVMe SSDs, bypassing the CPU bottleneck to dramatically reduce load times.
Windows 11 brought back a Widgets panel (replacing Live Tiles) providing a news feed, weather, calendar, and other personalized information in a sliding panel accessed from the taskbar.
Microsoft made a landmark $1 billion investment in OpenAI in 2019, followed by additional multi-billion dollar commitments in 2021 and 2023, gaining exclusive access to GPT models for its products.
In February 2023, Microsoft launched a new AI-powered Bing search engine and Edge browser sidebar powered by a customized version of OpenAI's GPT-4, under the name "Bing Chat."
Microsoft introduced Microsoft Copilot as a unified AI assistant brand in 2023, integrating AI capabilities across Windows, Microsoft 365, Edge, and Bing.

Windows 11 received a built-in Copilot button on the taskbar in September 2023 (via update), giving users direct access to an AI assistant that could change system settings, summarize content, and answer questions.

In May 2024, Microsoft unveiled the Copilot+ PC category, requiring a Neural Processing Unit (NPU) capable of at least 40 TOPS, enabling on-device AI features like Recall, Live Captions with real-time translation, and AI image generation.

Microsoft's Recall feature, announced for Copilot+ PCs in 2024, uses AI to create a searchable "photographic memory" of everything the user has done on their PC, though it faced significant privacy scrutiny before launch.
Windows PowerShell 1.0 was released in November 2006, created by Jeffrey Snover, providing a command-line shell and scripting language built on the .NET Framework for system administration.
In August 2016, Microsoft open-sourced PowerShell and released it cross-platform for Windows, macOS, and Linux, renaming it "PowerShell Core" (later just "PowerShell").
Microsoft launched the .NET Framework in 2002 as a managed code platform supporting multiple programming languages (C#, VB.NET, F#), simplifying Windows application development.
In 2016, Microsoft released .NET Core, a cross-platform, open-source reimplementation of .NET that runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux, later consolidated as ".NET" from version 5 (2020) onward.

Microsoft released Visual Studio Code (VS Code) in April 2015 as a free, open-source, cross-platform code editor; it has consistently ranked as the world's most popular code editor in developer surveys since 2018.
FAT32, introduced with Windows 95 OSR2 in 1996, extended the original FAT file system to support drives larger than 2 GB but has a maximum file size of 4 GB.
NTFS (New Technology File System) debuted with Windows NT 3.1 in 1993 and introduced file-level security, compression, encryption, journaling, and support for large volumes.
Microsoft introduced the Resilient File System (ReFS) with Windows Server 2012 as a successor to NTFS, designed for maximum data integrity and scalability in storage server workloads.
Microsoft Solitaire was included with Windows 3.0 in 1990 not just for entertainment but to teach users how to use a mouse - particularly the click-and-drag action.
Microsoft Paint debuted with Windows 1.0 in 1985 as a licensed version of ZSoft's PC Paintbrush; it has shipped with every version of Windows since.
Minesweeper was included with Windows 3.1 in 1992, created to help users practice precise mouse movements - specifically right-clicking and left-clicking.
Microsoft skipped from Windows 8.1 (kernel version 6.3) directly to Windows 10 (kernel version 10.0), partly for marketing reasons, and later skipped Windows 9 entirely.

One suspected technical reason Microsoft skipped Windows 9 is that many software programs checked for older Windows versions using code like "startsWith('Windows 9')" which would have matched "Windows 95" and "Windows 98," causing compatibility errors. This speculation was debunked.
Microsoft became the world's first publicly traded company to reach a $1 trillion market capitalization in April 2019.
Microsoft launched Bing as its rebranded search engine in June 2009, replacing Live Search, with the aim of positioning it as a "decision engine" rather than a traditional search tool.
Windows Update launched in 1998 with Windows 98 as an online service for downloading patches and updates, eliminating the need for users to obtain physical service pack media.
Internet Explorer reached peak market dominance of approximately 95% browser market share around 2003 before declining due to competition from Firefox, Chrome, and Safari.
Microsoft officially retired Internet Explorer on June 15, 2022, ending its 27-year run; it had fallen to a negligible market share by that point.
Microsoft Teams was launched in November 2016 as a workplace chat and collaboration platform, growing massively during the COVID-19 pandemic to reach 300 million monthly active users by 2023.

In 2014, then-CEO Satya Nadella declared "Microsoft loves Linux," a dramatic reversal from Steve Ballmer's 2001 description of Linux as a "cancer" - reflecting a fundamental shift in Microsoft's corporate philosophy toward open-source collaboration.
Microsoft launched the Windows Insider Program in October 2014, allowing millions of users to test pre-release Windows builds, forming a large feedback loop for OS development.
Notepad remained largely unchanged from Windows 1.0 until Windows 10 (version 1809, 2018), when it finally received support for Unix-style line endings and other modern text encoding features.

The Ctrl+Alt+Delete key combination was created by IBM engineer David Bradley for the IBM PC in 1981 as a hard reboot shortcut; Microsoft later repurposed it in Windows NT as a secure attention sequence for login.
Bill Gates wrote his first computer program at age 13 on a PDP-10 at Lakeside School in Seattle - a version of Tic-Tac-Toe that allowed a user to play against the computer.
Product Activation was introduced with Windows XP in 2001 to combat software piracy; it required users to activate their copy of Windows within 30 days of installation, either online or by phone.
Windows Task Manager was introduced in Windows NT 4.0 in 1996; it was created by David Plummer, a Microsoft developer, who wrote it in his spare time.
The Windows Registry was introduced with Windows 3.1 in 1992, replacing the fragmented .INI file system with a centralized hierarchical database for configuration settings.
The Recycle Bin was introduced with Windows 95 in 1995, replacing the earlier "Delete" command that permanently removed files, giving users a safety net to recover accidentally deleted items.
Microsoft launched its professional certification program (MCSE - Microsoft Certified Systems Engineer) in 1992, which became one of the world's most recognized IT credentials.

Microsoft's cloud storage service launched in 2007 as "Windows Live Folders," was renamed SkyDrive in 2008, and then renamed OneDrive in 2014 following a trademark dispute with British Sky Broadcasting.

Windows Defender began as Microsoft AntiSpyware in 2005, was rebranded as Windows Defender in 2006, and expanded into a full antivirus solution integrated into Windows 8, eventually becoming Microsoft Defender Antivirus.

Microsoft launched Xbox Game Pass in June 2017, a subscription service for Xbox and PC games; it expanded to include day-one releases of all first-party Microsoft titles, rapidly growing to tens of millions of subscribers.
Microsoft unveiled the HoloLens mixed reality headset in January 2015 - the world's first self-contained holographic computer - running a full version of Windows 10.

Windows Preinstallation Environment (WinPE) was introduced with Windows XP in 2001 as a lightweight bootable version of Windows used for deploying, troubleshooting, and recovering Windows installations.

Despite heavy investment, Bing has consistently held roughly 3-9% of the global search market share, compared to Google's dominance above 90%, though the 2023 AI-powered Bing refresh improved its user engagement significantly.

Clippy (officially "Clippit"), the animated paperclip Office Assistant, was introduced with Microsoft Office 97 in 1996 and was widely regarded as annoying, leading to its default disabling in Office 2002 and removal in Office 2007.
Microsoft launched Virtual Earth in 2005, which became Bing Maps in 2009, providing satellite imagery, street maps, and route planning - competing directly with Google Maps.

Windows Subsystem for Linux 2 (WSL 2), released in May 2020 with Windows 10 version 2004, replaced the translation layer with a real Linux kernel running in a lightweight VM, dramatically improving file system performance and compatibility.

Microsoft released Windows Terminal in 2019 as a modern, open-source terminal application supporting multiple tabs, GPU-accelerated text rendering, Unicode, and custom themes - replacing the aging Console Host.
Active Directory Domain Services (AD DS) was introduced with Windows 2000 Server in 1999 and became the de-facto identity and access management solution for enterprise Windows networks worldwide.
Windows XP Embedded (XPe) was widely used in ATMs, point-of-sale terminals, medical devices, and industrial systems well into the 2020s, long after mainstream consumer support ended.
Microsoft introduced "Patch Tuesday" in October 2003, standardizing the release of security patches to the second Tuesday of each month, helping IT administrators plan update deployments.
Consumer versions of Windows released from the 1990s onward include: 3.0, 3.1, 95, 98, Me, XP, Vista, 7, 8, 8.1, 10, and 11 - spanning over three decades of continuous development.

Microsoft announced the Pluton security chip architecture in 2020, a CPU-integrated security processor that moves security functionality directly into the silicon to address firmware attack vectors, first appearing in Windows 11 devices in 2022.
Microsoft announced that Windows 10 will reach end of support on October 14, 2025, after which it will no longer receive security updates - urging users to upgrade to Windows 11.
Press Win+D to instantly minimize all open windows and show the desktop. Press it again to restore the windows to their previous positions.
Press Win+L to immediately lock your PC and return to the lock screen, useful for quickly securing your workstation when stepping away.
Press Win+Tab to open Task View, where you can see all open windows, switch between virtual desktops, and view your timeline (Windows 10) or recent activities.
Press Win+Ctrl+D to create a new virtual desktop instantly. Use Win+Ctrl+Left/Right arrows to switch between them and Win+Ctrl+F4 to close the current one.
Press Win+Left or Win+Right to snap the active window to the left or right half of the screen. Use Win+Up or Win+Down to maximize or minimize it.
Press Win+E to open a new File Explorer window quickly from anywhere in Windows.
Press Win+R to open the Run dialog box, where you can quickly launch applications, open folders, or run commands such as "msconfig," "regedit," or "mmc."
Press Win+X (or right-click the Start button) to access the Power User menu, providing quick links to Device Manager, PowerShell, Disk Management, Task Manager, and more.
Press Win+I to open the Windows Settings app directly, which is faster than navigating through the Start menu.
Press Win+P to open the Project panel for quickly switching between display modes: PC screen only, Duplicate, Extend, or Second screen only.
Press Win+Shift+S to open the Snipping Tool (formerly Snip and Sketch) overlay, allowing you to capture a rectangular, freeform, window, or full-screen screenshot that is copied to the clipboard.
Press Win+V to open Clipboard History, which stores the last 25 items you've copied. Enable it first via Settings > System > Clipboard > Clipboard history.
Press Win+. (period) or Win+; (semicolon) to open the emoji picker panel, allowing you to insert emoji, GIFs, kaomoji, and special characters into any text field.
Press Alt+F4 to close the currently active application window. When no application is focused (on the desktop), it opens the Shut Down Windows dialog.
Press Win+Pause/Break to immediately open the System properties page, showing your Windows edition, CPU, RAM, and PC name - useful for quickly checking system specs.
Press Ctrl+Shift+Esc to open Task Manager directly, bypassing the Ctrl+Alt+Delete security screen and saving a step when diagnosing performance issues.
Press Win+Z on Windows 11 to open the Snap Layouts overlay for the current window, letting you choose a predefined multi-window arrangement without hovering over the maximize button.
Press Win+1 through Win+0 to launch or switch to the application pinned to the corresponding position on the taskbar, numbered left to right.
Press Win+Home to minimize all windows except the currently active one - a quick way to focus on a single application without moving other windows manually.

fact.topic: Win+Shift+Left/Right - Move window to other monitor
fact.text: Press Win+Shift+Left or Win+Shift+Right to move the active window to the next monitor in a multi-monitor setup, keeping the window's size and position relative to that display.
Click in the File Explorer address bar to type a path directly, or press Alt+D to select it. Type a folder path and press Enter to navigate there instantly.

In File Explorer, go to View > Show > File name extensions (Windows 11) or View > Options > Change folder and search options > View tab (Windows 10) to toggle visibility of file extensions for all files.
In File Explorer on Windows 11, go to View > Show > Hidden items to toggle hidden files and folders. On Windows 10, use View > Hidden items checkbox in the ribbon.
Enable item check boxes in File Explorer (View > Show > Item check boxes on Windows 11) to select files by clicking their checkboxes without holding Ctrl, which is especially helpful on touchscreens.
Right-click any file or folder in File Explorer and select Send to > Compressed (zipped) folder to create a ZIP archive of the selected items without needing third-party software.
Drag any folder to the Quick Access section in the File Explorer navigation pane (or right-click it and select Pin to Quick access) to add it as a permanent shortcut for fast navigation.
Open multiple File Explorer windows to the same or different folders and use Win+Left/Right or drag them side by side for easy file comparison or drag-and-drop between locations.
Windows 11 (22H2 and later) supports tabs in File Explorer - press Ctrl+T to open a new tab and Ctrl+W to close the current one, allowing multi-location browsing in a single window.
Press Alt+Up arrow in File Explorer to navigate to the parent folder of the current directory, equivalent to clicking the up-arrow button in the navigation bar.
Click on any part of the breadcrumb path in the File Explorer address bar (the folder icons between the ">" separators) to jump directly to that ancestor folder in the hierarchy.
Open Task Manager (Ctrl+Shift+Esc) > Startup tab to see all programs that launch at Windows startup and disable unnecessary ones to reduce boot time and improve startup performance.

Search for "Adjust the appearance and performance of Windows" in the Start menu and select "Adjust for best performance" to disable all visual effects, significantly improving performance on low-end hardware.

In Control Panel > Hardware and Sound > Power Options, select the "High performance" plan (or create a custom one) to prevent Windows from throttling your CPU for energy savings during demanding tasks.

Enable Storage Sense in Settings > System > Storage to automatically free up disk space by removing temporary files, old Windows Update files, and items in the Recycle Bin that have been there for a set number of days.

On systems with limited RAM and a traditional HDD, you can use a USB flash drive as a cache via ReadyBoost: right-click the drive in File Explorer > Properties > ReadyBoost tab and configure the space to use.
Run "cleanmgr /sageset:1" in an elevated prompt to configure what Disk Cleanup removes, then "cleanmgr /sagerun:1" to execute it silently - useful in scheduled maintenance scripts.

Open an elevated PowerShell prompt and run "Get-PhysicalDisk | Get-StorageReliabilityCounter | Select-Object DeviceId, ReadErrorsTotal, WriteErrorsTotal, Temperature" to check storage health statistics.
Enable Game Mode in Settings > Gaming > Game Mode to prioritize CPU and GPU resources for the active game, reducing background process interference during gaming sessions.

Hibernate saves the memory state to disk and powers off completely (using zero power), while Sleep keeps the session in RAM (using a small amount of power). Use Hibernate for overnight pauses on laptops to maximize battery life.

Windows Fast Startup (Settings > Power & sleep > Additional power settings > Choose what the power buttons do > Turn on fast startup) uses a partial hibernate on shutdown to speed up boot times significantly.
Open Windows Security (search for it in the Start menu) to see the status of your antivirus, firewall, account protection, device security, and app/browser control - your central security dashboard.
Set up Windows Hello in Settings > Accounts > Sign-in options to use face recognition, fingerprint, or a PIN for faster and more secure sign-in than a traditional password.

Search for "Change User Account Control settings" and adjust the slider to at least "Notify me only when apps try to make changes" to maintain a security baseline while reducing prompts for trusted actions.

Go to Windows Security > App & browser control > Exploit protection settings to configure system-level mitigations like DEP (Data Execution Prevention), ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization), and CFG (Control Flow Guard).

Enable Controlled folder access in Windows Security > Virus & threat protection > Ransomware protection to block unauthorized apps from modifying files in protected folders like Documents and Pictures.

Enable BitLocker on Windows 10/11 Pro, Enterprise, or Education by right-clicking a drive in File Explorer and selecting "Turn on BitLocker" to encrypt the entire drive and protect data if the device is lost or stolen.
Run "Get-BitLockerVolume" in an elevated PowerShell prompt to see the encryption status, protection status, and key protectors for all volumes on the system.
Open "Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security" (run "wf.msc") to create granular inbound and outbound firewall rules by program, port, protocol, IP address, or network profile.
Run "secpol.msc" to open the Local Security Policy editor, where you can enforce password complexity requirements, account lockout policies, audit policies, and other security settings.
Enable Dynamic Lock in Settings > Accounts > Sign-in options > Dynamic lock to automatically lock your PC when a paired Bluetooth device (like your phone) moves out of Bluetooth range.
Run "Get-ComputerInfo" in PowerShell to retrieve comprehensive system information including OS version, BIOS version, CPU, memory, domain membership, and Windows product ID in a single command.
Run "Get-Service | Where-Object {$_.Status -eq 'Running'}" in PowerShell to list all currently running Windows services, which is useful for auditing or troubleshooting service states.

Run "Get-ChildItem -Path C:\ -Recurse -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Where-Object {$_.Length -gt 100MB} | Sort-Object Length -Descending | Select-Object FullName, Length" in PowerShell to find files larger than 100 MB on the C: drive.

Run "Restart-Service -Name 'Spooler'" in an elevated PowerShell prompt to restart the Print Spooler service (replace 'Spooler' with any service name). Faster and scriptable compared to the Services GUI.
Run "Get-PSDrive -PSProvider FileSystem" in PowerShell to see all file system drives with their Used, Free, and Root information in a clean tabular format.
Run "Get-Process | Export-Csv -Path "$env:USERPROFILE\Desktop\processes.csv" -NoTypeInformation" in PowerShell to export a snapshot of all running processes to a CSV file on the desktop.

Run "Test-NetConnection -ComputerName google.com -Port 443" in PowerShell for a detailed network test that shows ping results, TCP connection success, route information, and whether the specified port is open.
Run "Get-Package | Sort-Object Name | Format-Table Name, Version, Source" in PowerShell to list all installed packages/applications with their versions - useful for auditing software inventory.

Use the Windows Package Manager command "winget install --id Microsoft.VisualStudioCode" (or any app ID) in PowerShell or Terminal to download and install apps silently from the Microsoft Store or web.

Run "Set-ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned -Scope CurrentUser" in PowerShell to allow locally created scripts to run while requiring scripts downloaded from the internet to be signed by a trusted publisher.

Run "Get-ScheduledTask | Where-Object {$_.State -eq 'Ready'} | Select-Object TaskName, TaskPath" in PowerShell to list all enabled scheduled tasks, helpful for auditing or identifying potentially unwanted tasks.

Use PowerShell to bulk rename files: "Get-ChildItem -Path C:\Photos -Filter *.jpg | Rename-Item -NewName { $_.Name -replace 'IMG_','Photo_' }" renames all .jpg files replacing "IMG_" with "Photo_" in a single operation.
Run "(Get-Date) - (gcim Win32_OperatingSystem).LastBootUpTime" in PowerShell to see how long your Windows system has been running since the last boot, expressed in days, hours, minutes, and seconds.
Press Win+R, type "regedit," and press Enter to open the Registry Editor. Always back up a registry key before modifying it by going to File > Export with the key selected.

To disable Windows Spotlight lock screen suggestions (ads), navigate to HKCU\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CloudContent and create a DWORD value "DisableWindowsSpotlightFeatures" set to 1. This disables lock screen advertisements.
To disable web/Bing results in the Start menu search, navigate to HKCU\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Explorer and create a DWORD "DisableSearchBoxSuggestions" set to 1.

Navigate to HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control and lower the "WaitToKillServiceTimeout" DWORD value (default 5000 milliseconds) to reduce the time Windows waits for services to stop during shutdown.

fact.topic: Add "Open command prompt here" to context menu
fact.text: Add a "Open PowerShell window here" option to the right-click context menu of any folder by navigating to HKCR\Directory\shell, creating a new key named "Open PowerShell here," adding an "icon" value of "powershell.exe" and a subkey "command" with the value: powershell.exe -noexit -command Set-Location '%V'

To disable Cortana, navigate to HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Windows Search and create or set the DWORD "AllowCortana" to 0. This prevents Cortana from running and removes it from the search bar.

Navigate to HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System and create a DWORD "verbosestatus" set to 1 to see detailed status messages during Windows startup, shutdown, logon, and logoff.
Navigate to HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer and create a DWORD "NoLowDiskSpaceChecks" set to 1 to suppress the low disk space balloon notification.

Navigate to HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\CrashControl and set the DWORD "AutoReboot" to 0 so that Windows displays the Blue Screen of Death (BSOD) indefinitely rather than automatically restarting, giving you time to read the error.

While the default browser is best set through Settings > Apps > Default apps, its registered ProgID is stored under HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\Shell\Associations\UrlAssociations\http\UserChoice - viewing this can help diagnose default app conflicts.

fact.topic: Prevent programs from startup via registry
fact.text: Programs that auto-start for the current user are registered under HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run. Delete any entry in this key to prevent that program from starting automatically on login.
Navigate to HKCU\Control Panel\Desktop\WindowMetrics and set the string value "MinAnimate" to "0" to disable window minimize/maximize animations, making the UI feel snappier on slower machines.

Run "ipconfig /flushdns" in an elevated Command Prompt or PowerShell to clear the DNS resolver cache, useful when you're experiencing DNS resolution issues or visiting a site after a DNS record change.

Run "ipconfig /release" followed by "ipconfig /renew" in an elevated prompt to release your current DHCP lease and obtain a new IP address from the DHCP server - useful for resolving IP conflict issues.
Run "netsh int ip reset" in an elevated prompt to reset the TCP/IP stack to its default state, resolving issues caused by corrupted network settings without reinstalling Windows.
Run "netsh winsock reset" in an elevated prompt to reset the Windows Sockets API configuration to its default state, which can fix connectivity issues caused by malware or corrupted socket settings.
Run "netstat -ano" in a prompt to display all active TCP/UDP connections with their local/remote addresses, ports, connection state, and the PID (Process ID) of the owning process.
Run "net use Z: \\server\share /persistent:yes" in an elevated prompt to map a network share to drive letter Z and make the mapping persist across reboots.

Go to Settings > System > Troubleshoot > Other troubleshooters > Internet Connections (Windows 11) or Settings > Update & Security > Troubleshoot > Internet Connections (Windows 10) to run the built-in network diagnostic wizard.

Run "netsh wlan show profile name="YourNetworkName" key=clear" in an elevated prompt to display the saved Wi-Fi password for any network your PC has previously connected to, shown under "Key Content."

In Settings > Network & Internet > select your connection > DNS server assignment (Windows 11), or through Network adapter properties > IPv4 > DNS, set your preferred DNS to a public DNS like 1.1.1.1 (Cloudflare) or 8.8.8.8 (Google) for potentially faster resolution.
Go to Settings > Network & Internet > Data usage to see how much data each app has used over the current month on both Wi-Fi and Ethernet - useful for tracking data-hungry applications.
Run "sfc /scannow" in an elevated prompt to scan all protected Windows system files and automatically repair any corrupted ones using cached copies from the Windows component store.

Run "DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth" in an elevated prompt to scan and repair the Windows component store (WinSxS), which SFC relies on - run DISM first if SFC reports it cannot repair files.
Run "eventvwr.msc" to open Event Viewer, your central log for Windows system, application, and security events - the first place to check when diagnosing crashes, service failures, or logon issues.
Open Resource Monitor by running "resmon.exe" or from Task Manager > Performance tab > Open Resource Monitor for detailed real-time graphs of CPU, memory, disk, and network usage per process.

Run "diskmgmt.msc" to open Disk Management and graphically manage drives, partitions, volumes, and drive letters - including creating, formatting, shrinking, or extending volumes without third-party tools.
Run "devmgmt.msc" to open Device Manager, where you can view, update, disable, or uninstall hardware drivers, and identify devices with driver problems (shown with a yellow exclamation mark).
Run "services.msc" to open the Services manager, where you can start, stop, pause, resume, and configure the startup type (Automatic, Manual, Disabled) of all Windows and third-party services.

Search for "View reliability history" in the Start menu to open Reliability Monitor - a graphical timeline of system crashes, application failures, and Windows updates, useful for diagnosing instability.
Run "msconfig" to open System Configuration, where you can manage boot options (Safe Mode, reduced memory), select which services start at boot, and configure advanced startup settings.
Run "perfmon.msc" to open Performance Monitor, which lets you graph hundreds of real-time counters for CPU, memory, disk, network, and more - powerful for diagnosing long-term performance trends.

Run "gpedit.msc" on Windows Pro and higher editions to access the Local Group Policy Editor, giving you administrative control over hundreds of OS behaviors, security settings, and app configurations without editing the registry manually.

Hold Shift while clicking Restart (from the Start menu or lock screen) to boot into the Windows Recovery Environment (WinRE), where you can access System Restore, Startup Repair, command prompt, and other recovery tools.

Go to Settings > System > Recovery > Reset this PC to reinstall Windows, with the option to keep your personal files or remove everything - useful for resolving deep OS corruption or preparing a device for resale.

Open Credential Manager (search Start menu or run "control keymgr.dll") to view, edit, and delete saved Windows credentials (network passwords, website credentials, and certificates) stored securely by Windows.
Run "taskschd.msc" to open Task Scheduler, where you can create automated tasks that run programs, scripts, or commands on a schedule, at logon, on idle, or in response to specific system events.
Use Win+Ctrl+D to create virtual desktops and Win+Ctrl+Left/Right to switch between them, allowing you to group related applications (work, browsing, communication) on separate desktops.

Enable Focus Assist (Settings > System > Focus assist on Windows 10, or Settings > System > Notifications > Focus on Windows 11) to suppress notifications during work or presentations, with options to allow priority contacts through.
Enable Night Light in Settings > System > Display > Night light to reduce blue light emission from your screen after sunset, potentially improving sleep quality if you work late.
Click and rapidly "shake" a window's title bar back and forth to minimize all other open windows to the taskbar, leaving only the shaken window visible on screen (Aero Shake).
Press Win+Plus (+) to launch the Magnifier tool and zoom in on any part of the screen. Press Win+Minus (-) to zoom out and Win+Esc to exit. Useful for accessibility or examining fine details.

Launch Sticky Notes from the Start menu to create digital sticky notes on your desktop; they sync across devices via your Microsoft account and integrate with Cortana (Windows 10) and the OneNote ecosystem.
After enabling Clipboard History (Win+V > Turn on), press Win+V before pasting to choose from the last 25 copied items rather than only the most recent one.
Windows 11's Snap Layouts support four equal quadrants - hover the maximize button, choose the four-quadrant layout, then place each window into a corner for a productive four-app split-screen view.
Middle-click a taskbar button (click with the scroll wheel) to open a new instance of that application - handy for launching a second File Explorer, browser window, or terminal.

On Windows 10, opening Task View (Win+Tab) also shows your activity timeline - a scrollable history of files and websites you've worked on over the past 30 days, synced with your Microsoft account across devices.
Press Left Alt+Left Shift+Print Screen to quickly toggle High Contrast mode on or off, making screen elements easier to see for users with visual impairments.
Press Win+Ctrl+Enter to toggle the Windows Narrator screen reader on or off. Narrator reads aloud screen content and is fully configurable in Settings > Accessibility > Narrator.
Run "osk.exe" or go to Settings > Accessibility > Keyboard > On-screen keyboard to open a fully functional virtual keyboard, useful if physical keyboard keys fail or for touchscreen use.
Press Win+H to open the voice dictation feature, which lets you speak text into any text field using the built-in speech recognition engine - no setup required in Windows 10 or 11.
Go to Settings > Accessibility > Mouse pointer and touch to change the cursor size and color (including a high-visibility black or custom color) to make it easier to locate on screen.

In Task Manager, click "More details" to expand to the full view. Right-click any column header to add additional columns such as Command line, Publisher, PID, or GPU usage for deeper process analysis.
The App History tab in Task Manager tracks cumulative CPU time and network usage by app since the last reset - useful for identifying which apps have consumed the most resources over time.
In Task Manager, go to Options > Always on top to keep it visible above all other windows - useful when monitoring performance metrics while running a demanding task.

In Windows 11's Task Manager, right-click a process and select "Efficiency mode" to lower its CPU priority and throttle it as a background task, reducing its impact on your system's performance and battery life.

In Task Manager > Details tab, right-click a process and select "Set affinity" to restrict which CPU cores it can run on - useful for isolating a process to certain cores for testing or compatibility reasons.

Right-click the desktop and select Display settings to drag and arrange the virtual positions of multiple monitors to match their physical layout on your desk, ensuring the mouse moves naturally between screens.
If you have an HDR-capable monitor, enable HDR in Settings > System > Display > HDR to allow Windows and compatible games and apps to use a wider color range and higher luminance levels.
Go to Settings > System > Display > Advanced display settings to select the maximum refresh rate supported by your monitor - ensuring you actually use 144 Hz or higher if your hardware supports it.
Go to Settings > System > Display > Scale and set a custom scaling percentage (100%, 125%, 150%, etc.) to make text and UI elements larger or smaller without changing the screen resolution.

Configure a screen saver (right-click desktop > Personalize > Lock screen > Screen saver settings) and check "On resume, display logon screen" to lock the PC automatically after a period of inactivity.

Enable File History in Settings > Update & Security > Backup (Windows 10) or Settings > System > Storage > Advanced storage settings > Backup (Windows 11) to automatically back up files in Libraries, Desktop, Contacts, and Favorites to an external drive.

Enable System Restore via "Create a restore point" in the Start menu, and create restore points before making major changes. If Windows becomes unstable, use System Restore to roll back system files and settings to a previous state.

Use Control Panel > Backup and Restore (Windows 7) > Create a system image to create a complete image backup of your Windows installation to an external drive or network location - enabling a full bare-metal restore.

Right-click any file or folder and select "Restore previous versions" to access shadow copies (Volume Shadow Copy) of files at previous points in time - useful for recovering accidentally deleted or overwritten files.

To access Windows Recovery Environment (WinRE) when Windows won't boot, restart the PC three times in a row during the boot process - Windows will automatically enter the recovery environment on the fourth attempt.
Right-click any application in the Start menu or search results and select "Pin to taskbar" to add it as a permanent button on the taskbar for quick one-click access.

On Windows 11, go to Settings > Personalization > Taskbar > Taskbar behaviors > Taskbar alignment and switch from "Center" to "Left" to restore the classic left-aligned Start button and taskbar icons.

Right-click the taskbar > Taskbar settings > Automatically hide the taskbar (in desktop mode) to give your screen more vertical space; the taskbar appears when you hover the mouse near the bottom of the screen.
Right-click any pinned or running app on the taskbar to open its Jump List, which shows recently opened files, frequent destinations, and app-specific quick actions without opening the app first.
On Windows 10, hover the mouse over the top or side edge of the Start menu until a resize cursor appears, then drag to make the Start menu taller or wider to show more tiles or app entries.

Run "wsl --install" in an elevated PowerShell prompt on Windows 10 (version 2004+) or Windows 11 to install WSL 2 and the default Ubuntu distribution in a single command, with no manual steps required.
Run "wsl --list --verbose" to see all installed WSL distributions, their current state (Running/Stopped), and their WSL version (1 or 2).
Access your Windows files from within WSL at the path "/mnt/c/" (for the C: drive). Conversely, access WSL files from Windows Explorer by typing "\\wsl$\Ubuntu" in the address bar.

Run "Enable-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online -FeatureName Microsoft-Hyper-V -All" in an elevated PowerShell prompt to enable Hyper-V on Windows 10/11 Pro, Enterprise, or Education without navigating through Control Panel.

Enable Windows Sandbox (run "Enable-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online -FeatureName Containers-DisposableClientVM -All" in elevated PowerShell, requires Pro/Enterprise) to run a temporary, isolated Windows environment that is destroyed when you close it - perfect for testing unknown software.
Run "winver" from Win+R or the Start menu to display a dialog showing your exact Windows build number, version, and edition - more precise than looking at System properties.
Run "wsl --set-default-version 2" to make WSL 2 the default for new distributions, and "wsl --set-version Ubuntu 2" to upgrade an existing Ubuntu installation from WSL 1 to WSL 2.

Run "winget upgrade --all" in PowerShell or Terminal to update all installed applications that have available updates through the Windows Package Manager, saving time versus updating apps individually.
Select "Microsoft Print to PDF" as the printer in any application's Print dialog to save the document as a PDF file natively in Windows - no third-party PDF software required.
Go to Settings > Bluetooth & devices (Windows 11) or Settings > Devices > Bluetooth (Windows 10/8.1) and toggle Bluetooth on, then click "Add device" to pair a new Bluetooth device.

Open Phone Link (formerly Your Phone) from the Start menu and link your Android phone (or iPhone on Windows 11) to see notifications, send messages, and mirror your phone's screen directly from your PC.
Press Win+S (or just start typing on Windows 10/11) to open Windows Search and search for apps, files, settings, and web results from a single box.
In Windows Search, type your query then click "Apps," "Documents," "Web," or "Settings" tabs to filter results to a specific category and find what you're looking for more quickly.
In File Explorer's search box, use operators like "kind:document," "datemodified:last week," "size:>1MB," or "ext:.pdf" to narrow file search results by type, date, size, or extension.
Go to Settings > Search > Searching Windows and switch from "Classic" to "Enhanced" indexing mode to have Windows index all folders on your PC for faster and more comprehensive file searches.

Go to Settings > Privacy & security (Windows 11) or Settings > Privacy (Windows 10) to review and restrict which apps have access to your camera, microphone, location, contacts, calendar, and other sensitive resources.
Go to Settings > Privacy & security > Diagnostics & feedback to switch diagnostic data from "Full" to "Required" (minimum), limiting the data Microsoft collects about your device usage.
Go to Settings > Privacy & security > Activity history and uncheck "Store my activity history on this device" to prevent Windows from logging your app and website activity for the Timeline feature.

Go to Settings > Privacy & security > General and turn off "Let apps show me personalized ads by using my advertising ID" to prevent apps from targeting you with personalized advertising based on your usage patterns.
In Settings > Privacy & security > Location, disable location services entirely or use the per-app toggle to control which specific apps are allowed to access your device's location.

Use "Select-String" in PowerShell as an equivalent to Unix grep: "Get-Content log.txt | Select-String 'error'" searches log.txt for lines containing "error," displaying them with filename and line number.

Run "Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='System'; Id=19} | Select-Object TimeCreated, Message | Format-List" in PowerShell to retrieve a history of successfully installed Windows updates from the System event log.

Run "Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Terminal Server' -Name 'fDenyTSConnections' -Value 0" in an elevated PowerShell prompt to enable Remote Desktop without opening the System Properties GUI.

fact.topic: Send a test email via PowerShell
fact.text: Use "Send-MailMessage -From 'a@example.com' -To 'b@example.com' -Subject 'Test' -Body 'Hello' -SmtpServer 'smtp.example.com'" in PowerShell to send an email directly from the command line - useful for testing mail relay configurations.

Run "powercfg /batteryreport /output %USERPROFILE%\Desktop\battery-report.html" in an elevated prompt to generate a detailed HTML report of your laptop's battery health, capacity history, and usage patterns.
Run "bcdedit | findstr /I winpe" or more reliably: run "Confirm-SecureBootUEFI" in PowerShell to check whether the system is using UEFI (returns True/False) instead of legacy BIOS.

Run "New-LocalUser -Name 'NewUser' -Password (ConvertTo-SecureString 'P@ssw0rd' -AsPlainText -Force) -FullName 'New User' -Description 'Test account'" in elevated PowerShell to create a new local user account programmatically.

fact.topic: Add user to local admin group
fact.text: Run "Add-LocalGroupMember -Group 'Administrators' -Member 'NewUser'" in an elevated PowerShell prompt to add a user to the local Administrators group without using the GUI.
Run "Compress-Archive -Path C:\Logs\* -DestinationPath C:\Backup\logs.zip" in PowerShell to zip an entire folder's contents into a .zip file natively, without third-party tools.
Run "Expand-Archive -Path C:\Downloads\file.zip -DestinationPath C:\Extracted" in PowerShell to extract a .zip archive to a specified folder without needing WinZip, 7-Zip, or other tools.

Create a registry key at HKCR\*\shell\Take Ownership with the value "(Default)" = "Take Ownership" and a subkey "command" with the value: powershell -command "Start-Process cmd -ArgumentList '/c takeown /f \"%1\" && icacls \"%1\" /grant administrators:F' -Verb RunAs" to add a one-click ownership transfer option to file context menus.

Navigate to HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System and create a DWORD "DisableLogonBackgroundImage" set to 1 to disable the default logon background and show a plain color, reducing logon screen load times.

Navigate to HKCR\CLSID\{018D5C66-4533-4307-9B53-224DE2ED1FE6} and set the DWORD "System.IsPinnedToNameSpaceTree" to 0 to hide the OneDrive icon from the File Explorer navigation pane without uninstalling OneDrive.

On Windows 10 Pro and higher, navigate to HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Personalization and create a DWORD "NoLockScreen" set to 1 to skip the lock screen and go directly to the sign-in screen on wake.

Navigate to HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer and create or modify the string value "Max Cached Icons" (set to "4096" or higher) to increase the icon cache, reducing blank icon appearances after restart.

fact.topic: Share a folder over the network
fact.text: Right-click a folder in File Explorer > Properties > Sharing tab > Advanced Sharing to configure share name, user permissions, and limits on simultaneous connections for network file sharing without a server OS.

Go to Settings > System > Remote Desktop (Windows 10/11) and toggle "Enable Remote Desktop" on to allow Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) connections to your PC from other Windows devices on the same network or via VPN.
Go to Settings > Network & internet > Mobile hotspot to turn your Windows PC into a Wi-Fi hotspot, sharing its internet connection with up to eight other devices over Wi-Fi or Bluetooth.

Run "Get-NetAdapter | Select-Object Name, Status, LinkSpeed" in PowerShell to see the connection speed (e.g., 1 Gbps, 100 Mbps) reported by each network adapter, confirming whether you're using your fastest available interface.

Run "tracert hostname" (e.g., "tracert google.com") or the PowerShell equivalent "Test-NetConnection -ComputerName google.com -TraceRoute" to trace the network path to a destination and identify where latency or packet loss occurs.

Run "Get-WindowsUpdate" in PowerShell (after installing the PSWindowsUpdate module via "Install-Module PSWindowsUpdate") to check for available Windows updates from the command line, enabling scriptable patch management.

Go to Settings > Windows Update > Advanced options > Pause updates to suspend Windows Update delivery for up to five weeks - useful when an update breaks critical software or during high-demand work periods.
Configure Active Hours in Settings > Windows Update > Advanced options > Active hours to tell Windows your working hours, preventing automatic restarts to install updates during that time window.

On Windows 10/11 Pro and higher, go to Settings > Windows Update > Advanced options and set the "Feature update deferral" to up to 365 days, installing only security patches while delaying major feature updates until they're more stable.

Enterprise and Server environments can configure Windows Update to pull updates from a local Windows Server Update Services (WSUS) server via Group Policy (gpedit.msc > Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Windows Update) to centralize and control update deployment.

Use "robocopy C:\Source D:\Destination /MIR /Z /LOG:C:\robocopy.log" to mirror a source folder to a destination with resume capability, deletion of orphaned files, and detailed logging - far more powerful than xcopy for backup scripts.
Use "sc.exe query type= all state= all" in an elevated prompt to list every service on the system (running and stopped) with its type and current state - a fast audit of all installed services.

While WMIC is deprecated in favor of PowerShell, "Get-WmiObject -Class Win32_Product | Select Name, Version" in PowerShell provides the same software inventory information using the modern supported interface.
Run "whoami /all" in a prompt to display your current username, security identifier (SID), group memberships, and privilege list - essential for diagnosing permission issues.
Run "cipher /w:C:\Path\To\Folder" to overwrite all free space in a folder with random data, making previously deleted files unrecoverable - a built-in alternative to third-party secure-delete tools.
Right-click the desktop > Personalize > Background and select "Slideshow," then choose a folder of images and set the change interval to create an automatic rotating desktop wallpaper.
Go to Settings > Personalization > Colors to choose a custom accent color for your Start menu, taskbar, window title bars, and UI highlights instead of the default blue.
Go to Settings > Personalization > Colors and set "Choose your mode" to "Dark" to enable system-wide dark mode for the Start menu, taskbar, Settings app, and apps that respect the system theme.
Go to Settings > Personalization > Themes to apply a complete theme that simultaneously changes the desktop background, accent color, lock screen, mouse cursor, and system sounds in one click.

Right-click the desktop > Personalize > Themes > Sounds to customize which sounds Windows plays for system events like login, error notifications, and new mail - or select "No Sounds" for a completely silent Windows experience.

fact.topic: Create a Hyper-V VM via PowerShell
fact.text: Run "New-VM -Name 'TestVM' -MemoryStartupBytes 2GB -NewVHDPath 'C:\VMs\TestVM.vhdx' -NewVHDSizeBytes 50GB -Generation 2" in an elevated PowerShell prompt to create a Generation 2 Hyper-V virtual machine without the GUI.

Run "Checkpoint-VM -VMName 'TestVM' -SnapshotName 'BeforeUpdate'" in elevated PowerShell to create a Hyper-V checkpoint (snapshot) of a VM before making changes, allowing instant rollback if something goes wrong.
Run "New-VMSwitch -Name 'ExternalSwitch' -NetAdapterName 'Ethernet' -AllowManagementOS $true" in elevated PowerShell to create a Hyper-V external virtual switch tied to a physical network adapter.

fact.topic: Get AD user info via PowerShell
fact.text: Run "Get-ADUser -Identity username -Properties *" in PowerShell (with RSAT/Active Directory module) to retrieve all attributes of an Active Directory user account, including last logon, account status, and group memberships.
Run "Search-ADAccount -LockedOut | Select-Object Name, SamAccountName, LockedOut" in PowerShell to list all currently locked-out Active Directory user accounts across the domain.
Run "Unlock-ADAccount -Identity username" in elevated PowerShell to unlock a locked-out Active Directory user account instantly, without navigating through Active Directory Users and Computers.

Run "gpupdate /force" in an elevated prompt on a domain-joined machine to immediately apply all Group Policy Objects (GPOs) from the domain, rather than waiting for the standard 90-minute refresh interval.

Run "gpresult /h C:\GPReport.html" in an elevated prompt to generate an HTML report of all Group Policy settings currently applied to the computer and logged-in user - invaluable for diagnosing policy application issues.

Run "repadmin /showrepl" in an elevated prompt on a domain controller to display the Active Directory replication status between all domain controllers, identifying any replication failures or delays.

fact.topic: Join a PC to a domain via PowerShell
fact.text: Run "Add-Computer -DomainName 'contoso.com' -Credential (Get-Credential) -Restart" in elevated PowerShell to join the local computer to an Active Directory domain and restart immediately to complete the process.

Open the Settings app > System > Storage > Advanced storage settings > Storage Spaces to create a software RAID (mirror or parity) pool from multiple drives - protecting your data from a single drive failure without dedicated RAID hardware.
Open Disk Management (diskmgmt.msc), right-click a volume and select "Shrink Volume" to reduce its size and create unallocated space - useful for creating a second partition without wiping data.
Run "Repair-Volume -DriveLetter C -Scan" in elevated PowerShell to scan the C: drive for file system errors (use -OfflineScanAndFix to repair, requiring a restart for the system drive).
Open "Defragment and Optimize Drives" (search the Start menu), select your SSD and click "Optimize" - Windows will run TRIM on SSDs instead of defragmentation, maintaining SSD performance over time.
Run "Format-Volume -DriveLetter D -FileSystem NTFS -NewFileSystemLabel 'DataDrive' -Confirm:$false" in elevated PowerShell to format a volume as NTFS with a custom label, non-interactively.
Press Win+G to open the Xbox Game Bar overlay while in a game or app, providing quick access to screen recording, screenshot, audio mixer, performance widgets, and Xbox Social features.
Press Win+Alt+G to capture the last 30 seconds of gameplay (or any app) using the Xbox Game Bar's background recording - no need to start recording in advance.

Enable Hardware-Accelerated GPU Scheduling (HAGS) in Settings > System > Display > Graphics settings > Change default graphics settings to reduce latency and improve GPU performance in supported games and on compatible graphics hardware.

Enable Auto HDR in Settings > System > Display > Graphics settings > Change default graphics settings to automatically apply HDR enhancement to DirectX 11/12 games that don't natively support HDR on Copilot+ PC or compatible HDR displays.

Enable Variable Refresh Rate in Settings > System > Display > Graphics settings > Change default graphics settings to allow games to use VRR (G-Sync/FreeSync) even in windowed mode on compatible displays and GPU drivers.
Press Win+A to open the Quick Settings panel (Action Center on Windows 10) for fast toggles of Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, airplane mode, Focus assist, screen brightness, and volume without opening Settings.
Click the notification bell icon in the taskbar (or press Win+N on Windows 11) to see your notification history - useful when notifications appear briefly and you miss them.

Create a new folder on the desktop and name it exactly "GodMode.{ED7BA470-8E54-465E-825C-99712043E01C}" to create a "God Mode" shortcut that provides access to all Windows control panel settings in a single window.

fact.topic: Run programs as a different user
fact.text: Hold Shift and right-click an executable or shortcut, then select "Run as different user" to launch the program under a different account's credentials without switching the entire Windows session.

Enable Nearby Sharing in Settings > System > Nearby sharing to wirelessly send files, links, and photos to other Windows 10/11 PCs in the vicinity via Bluetooth or Wi-Fi without requiring any cables or a shared network folder.

Press PrtSc to copy a screenshot of the entire screen to the clipboard, Alt+PrtSc to capture only the active window, or Win+PrtSc to save a full-screen screenshot directly to the Pictures\Screenshots folder.

Press Win+Ctrl+L on Windows 11 (22H2+) to enable Live Captions, which transcribes audio from any application or microphone in real time and displays it as subtitles at the top or bottom of the screen.

Press Ctrl+Alt+Arrow key (Up/Down/Left/Right) to rotate the screen orientation - useful for portrait displays or accidentally triggered, causing confusion. This is a display driver shortcut (Intel/AMD) and may not work on all systems.

Run "psr.exe" (Problem Steps Recorder) to open a built-in tool that records mouse clicks and keystrokes with annotated screenshots, saving the result as a ZIP of an MHTML file - ideal for documenting reproducible bugs to send to support.

Enable Voice Access in Settings > Accessibility > Speech > Voice access to fully control your PC with voice commands - clicking buttons, typing, scrolling, and navigating menus hands-free using a microphone.
Run "Get-TPM" in elevated PowerShell to see whether a Trusted Platform Module is present, enabled, activated, and what version it is (1.2 or 2.0) - key for Windows 11 upgrade eligibility checks.

Go to Settings > System > Power & battery > Energy recommendations on Windows 11 to see a list of settings changes (like screen timeout, sleep settings) that will reduce energy use, with one-click apply buttons.

fact.topic: Steps Recorder replacement - Snipping Tool video
fact.text: In Windows 11 (22H2+), open the Snipping Tool and switch to video mode (the camera icon) to record a portion of your screen to an MP4 file - a modern replacement for screen recording without third-party software.

Drag a .ttf or .otf font file onto the Settings > Personalization > Fonts page (or right-click the font file and select Install for all users) to install custom fonts system-wide - available to all applications immediately.

Go to Settings > Apps > Optional features to add or remove optional Windows components like Notepad, WordPad, the Windows Media Player, XPS Viewer, OpenSSH Client, or the SNMP protocol without needing installation media.

Windows 10 (version 1809+) and Server 2019+ include OpenSSH Client and Server as optional features. Install them via Settings > Apps > Optional features > Add a feature, then connect with "ssh user@hostname" directly from PowerShell or Terminal.

Create a ".wsb" configuration file (XML format) to launch Windows Sandbox with specific settings such as mapped shared folders, networking enabled/disabled, logon scripts, and virtual GPU support, enabling repeatable isolated test environments.

fact.topic: Assign a drive letter to a folder
fact.text: Run "subst Z: C:\LongPath\To\DeepFolder" to map a deeply nested folder to a virtual drive letter, making it easier to access from File Explorer and Command-line tools.

Go to Settings > Accounts > Sign-in options and enable "For improved security, only allow Windows Hello sign-in for Microsoft accounts on this device" to enforce passwordless authentication using biometrics or PIN only.

Go to Settings > Windows Update > Advanced options > Delivery Optimization to allow or restrict Windows from using your PC's bandwidth to deliver updates to other PCs on your network or the internet, reducing Microsoft's server load.
Hold Shift and right-click the PowerShell or Terminal button on the taskbar, then select "Run as administrator" to open an elevated prompt without navigating through the Start menu.

On Windows 11, open Quick Settings (Win+A) and click the pencil (Edit) icon to add, remove, and rearrange the shortcut tiles in the panel - customizing your Quick Settings layout to show only the toggles you use most.

Right-click the speaker icon in the system tray and select "Open volume mixer" to set independent volume levels for each running application - for example, lowering game volume while keeping Discord louder.

Go to Settings > Apps > Default apps, scroll down to "Set defaults by file type" to choose exactly which application opens each specific file extension, giving you precise control over .pdf, .mp4, .png, and other associations.

Go to Settings > System > Storage and click on any category (Apps & features, Temporary files, etc.) to see which folders or applications are consuming the most space, with direct links to manage or uninstall them.

Go to Settings > Personalization > Colors and enable "Automatically pick an accent color from my background" to have Windows dynamically select a complementary accent color every time your wallpaper changes.
Go to Settings > Time & language > Date & time and enable "Set time zone automatically" so Windows uses your location to automatically select the correct time zone when you travel with a laptop.

Search "Edit the system environment variables" in the Start menu to open the Environment Variables dialog and add directories to the system PATH variable, making command-line tools available from any location without specifying the full path.
Run "$env:PATH -split ';'" in PowerShell to display all directories in the current PATH environment variable as a clean list, one per line - useful for diagnosing missing-command errors.

On Windows 11 with Copilot enabled, press Win+C and type natural-language instructions like "Turn on dark mode" or "How do I increase font size?" - Copilot can directly change many system settings without requiring manual navigation through menus.

fact.topic: Rename multiple files in File Explorer
fact.text: Select multiple files in File Explorer, press F2, type a new name, and press Enter to rename all selected files at once - Windows automatically appends numbers (1), (2), etc., to each file to keep names unique.
